title: Common ancestry and continuing evolution
description: Test your knowledge of common ancestry and continuing evolution!

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements provides evidence that all of Earth’s organisms are descended from a common ancestor?**

👁️ **Note that there are 2 answers to this question.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Bacteria, eukaryotes, and archaea use DNA to encode proteins, which carry out cellular functions.
Bacteria, eukaryotes, and archaea use ATP to release energy that can be used by the cell.
Bacteria, eukaryotes, and archaea exist on every continent on Earth.
Bacteria, eukaryotes, and archaea can all be grown and studied under similar laboratory conditions.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following statements:

> A. Bacteria, eukaryotes, and archaea use DNA to encode proteins.

>B. All prokaryotes and eukaryotes can be grown and studied under similar laboratory conditions.

>C. Organisms from all domains use ATP to release energy that can be used by the cell.

**Which of the statements provide evidence that all of Earth’s organisms are descended from a common ancestor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Statement A only
Statements A and C
Statements B and C
Statements A, B, and C

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider the following statements:

>I. Eukaryotes and prokaryotes exist on every continent on Earth.

>II. Bacteria, eukaryotes, and archaea use ATP to power cell processes.

>III. Organisms from all domains use DNA to encode proteins, which carry out cellular functions.

**Which of the statements provide evidence that all of Earth’s organisms are descended from a common ancestor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Statement II only
Statement III only
Statements I and III
Statements II and III

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagrams represent the generation of mature mRNA in a eukaryotic cell (A) and a prokaryotic cell (B).

[[☃ image 1]]

**Of the structures depicted in the diagrams, which were likely present in the common ancestor of all eukaryotes, but not in the common ancestor of all organisms?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
To identify a set of cellular structures that were present in the common ancestor of all eukaryotes, but not in the common ancestor of all organisms, we have to identify structures that:

* *are* found in virtually all modern eukaryotes, and

* are *not* found in modern prokaryotes.

We can use the diagrams to help us identify structures present in modern eukaryotes but not in modern prokaryotes. 

The following structures are present in the eukaryotic cell diagram, and not in the prokaryotic cell diagram: spliceosomal introns (shown as the dark blue parts of the mRNA), membrane-bound organelles (including mitochondria and the nucleus), and linear chromosomes (shown inside the nucleus).

The correct answer is:

* spliceosomal introns, membrane-bound organelles, and linear chromosomes

options.alt: Two organisms. The first organism, labeled A, consists of a circle with a double wall. There is also an inner circle. Outside of the inner circle are multiple dots, 3 bean-shaped structures, and a curved structure with multiple holes and breaks in it. Inside the inner circle are 3 chromosomes. A line coming from one chromosome expands to show duplex D N A. An arrow coming from this duplex shows a single strand with portions of different colors. The sequence of colors from left to right is blue, purple, teal, purple, gray. Two arrows point from this strand to two shorter single strands. One single strand is blue then teal. The other single strand is blue then purple. The second organism, labeled B, consists of an oval with a long tail. Inside the oval are multiple dots and a long tangled line. A blowup of the tangled line shows duplex D N A. An arrow points from the duplex to a single strand.

choices.content: spliceosomal introns, membrane-bound organelles, and linear chromosomes
ribosomes, cytosol, and double-stranded DNA
a circular chromosome, a peptidoglycan cell wall, and a flagellum
RNA molecules, a cell membrane, and cytoplasm

assessment_items.question_content:
The following diagram represents the generation of mature mRNA in a prokaryotic cell (A) and a eukaryotic cell (B).

[[☃ image 1]]

**Of the structures depicted in the diagrams, which were likely present in the common ancestor of all eukaryotes, but not in the common ancestor of all organisms?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
To identify a set of cellular structures that were present in the common ancestor of all eukaryotes, but not in the common ancestor of all organisms, we have to identify structures that:

* *are* found in virtually all modern eukaryotes, and

* are *not* found in modern prokaryotes.

We can use the diagrams to help us identify structures present in modern eukaryotes but not in modern prokaryotes. 

The following structures are present in the eukaryotic cell diagram, and not in the prokaryotic cell diagram: linear chromosomes (shown inside the nucleus), spliceosomal introns (shown as the dark blue parts of the mRNA), and membrane-bound organelles (including mitochondria and the nucleus).

The correct answer is:

* linear chromosomes, spliceosomal introns, and membrane-bound organelles

options.alt: Two organisms. The first organism, labeled A, consists of an oval with a long tail. Inside the oval are multiple dots and a long tangled line. A blowup of the tangled line shows duplex D N A. An arrow points from the duplex to a single strand. The second organism, labeled B, consists of a circle with a double wall. There is also an inner circle. Outside of the inner circle are multiple dots, 3 bean-shaped structures, and a curved structure with multiple holes and breaks in it. Inside the inner circle are 3 chromosomes. A line coming from one chromosome expands to show duplex D N A. An arrow coming from this duplex shows a single strand with portions of different colors. The sequence of colors from left to right is blue, purple, teal, purple, gray. Two arrows point from this strand to two shorter single strands. One single strand is blue then teal. The other single strand is blue then purple.

choices.content: linear chromosomes, spliceosomal introns, and membrane-bound organelles
a peptidoglycan cell wall, a flagellum, and a circular chromosome
a cell membrane, ribosomes, and cytoplasm
cytosol, double-stranded DNA, and RNA molecules

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following describes the common ancestor of all eukaryotes, but not the common ancestor of all organisms?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To identify which single-celled organism best describes the common ancestor of all eukaryotes, but not the common ancestor of all organisms, we have to identify the single-celled organism with:

* structures that *are* found in virtually all modern eukaryotes, and

* structures that are *not* found in modern prokaryotes.
The following structures are found in modern eukaryotes and not in modern prokaryotes: membrane-bound organelles, linear chromosomes, and spliceosomal introns.

The correct answer is:

* a single-celled organism with membrane-bound organelles, linear chromosomes, and spliceosomal introns

choices.content: a single-celled organism with membrane-bound organelles, linear chromosomes, and spliceosomal introns
a single-celled organism with a flagellum, a circular chromosome, and a peptidoglycan cell wall
a single-celled organism with double-stranded DNA, cytoplasm, and ribosomes
a single-celled organism with mRNA molecules, a cell membrane, and cytosol

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table lists evolution rate estimates for a set of viruses. The evolution rates are measured as the number of base-pair substitutions per nucleotide site in the virus genome, per year (s/n/y).

Virus name | Evolution rate (s/n/y)
- | -
Norwalk virus | $4.97$ x $10^{-3}$
SARS coronavirus | $2.82$ x $10^{-3}$
Human rotavirus A | $1.66$ x $10^{-3}$
West Nile virus | $0.85$ x $10^{-3}$
Human hepatitis B virus | $0.43$ x $10^{-3}$
Human adenovirus B | $0.07$ x $10^{-3}$

**Which of the following claims is best supported by the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The SARS coronavirus is more likely to evolve antiviral drug resistance over a given time period than the human hepatitis B virus.
A vaccine against the human rotavirus A is likely to be more durable in the population than a vaccine against human adenovirus B.
The West Nile virus is more likely to evolve to have an expanded host range over a given time period than the Norwalk virus is.
The genome of the Norwalk virus is larger than the genome of the SARS coronavirus.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table lists evolution rate estimates for a set of viruses. The evolution rates are measured as the number of base-pair substitutions per nucleotide site in the virus genome, per year (s/n/y).

Virus name | Evolution rate (s/n/y)
- | -
Influenza A virus | $3.61$ x $10^{-3}$
Respiratory syncytial virus | $2.31$ x $10^{-3}$
Hepatitis C virus | $1.39$ x $10^{-3}$
Measles virus | $0.67$ x $10^{-3}$
Human parvovirus B19 | $0.18$ x $10^{-3}$
Varicella zoster virus | $0.01$ x $10^{-3}$

**Which of the following claims is best supported by the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A vaccine against the measles virus is likely to be more durable in the population than a vaccine against the influenza A virus.
The human parvovirus B19 is more likely to evolve antiviral drug resistance than the respiratory syncytial virus.
The hepatitis C virus is more likely to evolve to have an expanded host range over a given time period than the influenza A virus is.
The genome of the hepatitis C virus is larger than the genome of the varicella zoster virus.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following table lists evolution rate estimates for a set of plant viruses. The evolution rates are measured as the number of base-pair substitutions per nucleotide site in the virus genome, per year (s/n/y).

Virus name | Evolution rate (s/n/y)
- | -
Beet chlorosis virus | $2.70$ x $10^{-3}$
Barley yellow dwarf virus | $1.50$ x $10^{-3}$
Sugarcane yellow leaf virus | $1.20$ x $10^{-3}$
Turnip yellows virus | $1.00$ x $10^{-3}$
Maize streak virus | $0.39$ x $10^{-3}$
Banana bunchy top virus | $0.14$ x $10^{-3}$

**Which of the following claims is best supported by the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The barley yellow dwarf virus is more likely to overcome engineered virus resistance in plants than the maize streak virus is.
The banana bunchy top virus is more likely to evolve an expanded host range over a given time period than the turnip yellows virus is.
The maize streak virus is more likely to cause severe disease symptoms than the beet chlorosis virus is.
The genome of the beet chlorosis virus is larger than the genome of the sugarcane yellow leaf virus.

assessment_items.question_content:
Weeds growing among crop plants can cause significant losses in crop yields. One way that farmers can remove weeds from their fields is through the use of herbicides. 

One class of herbicides, the acetolactate synthase (ALS)-inhibitors, target the enzyme ALS. Normally, the ALS enzyme carries out the first step of valine, leucine, and isoleucine synthesis. However, when a plant’s ALS enzymes are inhibited, the plant cannot make these amino acids and dies.

**Which of the following best predicts what will happen to a weed population in a field that is, over time, consistently sprayed with an ALS-inhibiting herbicide?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The weed population will evolve resistance to the herbicide, likely through selection of plants with a mutation in the *ALS* gene.
The weed population will evolve resistance to the herbicide, likely through selection of plants that can grow without valine, leucine, and isoleucine.
The weed population will be controlled by the herbicide because a population in a single field is too small to evolve resistance to an herbicide.
The weed population will be controlled by the herbicide because plant generation times are too long to evolve resistance to an herbicide.

assessment_items.question_content:
Penicillin and other beta-lactam antibiotics are routinely given to farm animals at low doses to prevent infection. These antibiotics cause bacterial cell death by binding to cell wall-synthesis proteins, including the protein PBP2. This binding inhibits proper cell wall synthesis, and causes the cell to burst.

**Which of the following best predicts what will happen to a population of bacteria in a farm setting that is, over time, consistently exposed to beta-lactam antibiotics?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The bacteria population will evolve resistance to the antibiotics, likely through selection of individuals with a mutation in the *PBP2* gene.
The bacteria population will evolve resistance to the antibiotics, likely through selection of individuals who don’t need a cell wall to grow and reproduce.
All of the bacteria in the population will be killed by the antibiotic because individuals in the population are genetically identical and equally susceptible to the antibiotic.
None of the bacteria in the population will be killed by the antibiotic because the farm animals will evolve antibiotic resistance, causing the antibiotic to lose its effectiveness.

assessment_items.question_content:
Pink bollworms are a major pest of cotton, causing huge crop losses around the world. To prevent pink bollworm infestation, some farmers grow cotton plants that have been engineered to produce the insecticide Bt. 

When a bollworm feeds on a Bt-producing cotton plant, the Bt enters the insect’s midgut and binds to proteins called cadherins. This binding damages the insect’s midgut, eventually causing death.

**Which of the following best predicts what will happen over time to a pink bollworm population in a field that is planted entirely with Bt-producing cotton plants?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The pink bollworm population will evolve resistance to Bt, likely through selection of bollworms that can avoid ingesting Bt.
The pink bollworm population will evolve resistance to Bt, likely through selection of bollworms with a mutation in the cadherin gene.
The pink bollworm population will be controlled by the insecticide because a population in a single field is too small to evolve resistance to an insecticide.
The pink bollworm population will be controlled by the insecticide because insect generation times are too short to evolve resistance to an insecticide.
